---
title: "Conventions and notation"
---

## The fixed-frame amplitude {#sec-fixed-frame-def}

The central object of this report is the decay matrix element written as a
concrete function of four-vectors,
$$
M_{\lambda_0;\,\lambda_1\dots\lambda_n}(p_1,\dots,p_n),
$$
for $0\to 1\,2\,\dots n$, with the following definitions held fixed
throughout:

- One global coordinate system $(\hat x,\hat y,\hat z)$ in the rest frame of
  the parent. The parent spin projection $\lambda_0$ is quantized along
  $\hat z$.
- The helicity $\lambda_i$ of final-state particle $i$ is its spin projection
  onto its own momentum direction $\hat p_i$ *in this common frame*, with the
  one-particle helicity state built by one fixed convention, e.g.
  $$
  |p,\lambda\rangle
  = U\!\left[R(\phi_p,\theta_p,-\phi_p)\right]
    U\!\left[L_z(|\vec p\,|)\right] |s,\lambda\rangle .
  $$
  The alternative convention $R(\phi,\theta,0)$ differs by a phase
  $e^{i\lambda\phi_p}$; either is acceptable as long as **one convention is
  used for every particle**. Symmetry statements derived below are valid for
  any single such choice (see the robustness remarks in each chapter).
- Configurations with $\vec p$ exactly along $-\hat z$ are a measure-zero set
  where the polar-angle convention is ambiguous; they are excluded from
  pointwise numerical checks.

This object is *not* what fitters typically evaluate — but it is where the
discrete symmetries act simply. Everything else is derived from it.

## Chain (aligned) amplitudes

Cascade models are built from decay chains. For a three-body decay
$0\to(ij)k$ through an isobar $R$,
$$
A^{(k)}_{\lambda_0;\,\{\lambda\}}(\sigma_{ij}, \Omega\text{'s})
= \sum_{\tau}
H^{0\to R k}_{\tau,\lambda_k}\,
D^{J_0\,*}_{\lambda_0,\,\tau-\lambda_k}(\Omega_k)\;
\mathrm{BW}_R(\sigma_{ij})\,
H^{R\to ij}_{\lambda_i,\lambda_j}\,
D^{J_R\,*}_{\tau,\,\lambda_i-\lambda_j}(\Omega_{ij}),
$$
schematically. Two features cause all the trouble:

1. **Frame proliferation.** The helicities $\lambda_i,\lambda_j$ inside the
   chain are defined in the isobar rest frame reached by a *chain-specific*
   sequence of boosts. Different chains define different frames for the same
   particle; relating them requires Wigner rotations (the "alignment" problem).
2. **Aligned kinematics.** In the Dalitz-plot decomposition (DPD)
   [@JPAC:2019ufm] the overall rotation is factored out and the remaining
   dynamic function $O^{\lambda_0}_{\{\lambda\}}(\sigma_1,\sigma_2,\sigma_3)$
   is evaluated in a *standard orientation* tied to one chosen chain. A
   particle permutation moves the configuration out of the standard
   orientation; bringing it back generates the permutation phases.

The report treats the fixed-frame amplitude and the aligned amplitude as two
representations of the same object, connected by explicit rotations:
$$
M_{\lambda_0;\{\lambda\}}(p_1,\dots,p_n)
= \sum_{\{\nu\}} \left[\text{alignment rotations}\right]^{\{\nu\}}_{\{\lambda\}}
\; O^{\lambda_0}_{\{\nu\}}(\sigma_1,\dots).
$$

## Two-body vertices

For a decay $R\to 1\,2$ ($J\to s_1, s_2$) the helicity couplings
$H^{R\to12}_{\lambda_1\lambda_2}$ relate to LS couplings by the standard
Jacob–Wick recoupling [@JacobWick1959; @Chung:1971ri]
$$
H^{R\to 12}_{\lambda_1\lambda_2}
= \sum_{LS} g_{LS}\,
\sqrt{\frac{2L+1}{2J+1}}\,
\langle L\,0\,;S\,\lambda | J\,\lambda\rangle\,
\langle s_1\,\lambda_1\,;s_2\,{-\lambda_2} | S\,\lambda\rangle,
\qquad \lambda=\lambda_1-\lambda_2 .
$$

Parity conservation at the vertex implies the Jacob–Wick relation
$$
H_{-\lambda_1,-\lambda_2}
= \eta_R\,\eta_1\,\eta_2\,(-1)^{J-s_1-s_2}\,
H_{\lambda_1\lambda_2}.
$$

Both formulas embed the Jacob–Wick "particle-2" convention. Any statement in
this report that depends on them inherits that convention; the cross-check
chapter explains how to detect a mismatch numerically.

## Clebsch–Gordan symmetries used throughout

With Condon–Shortley phases:
$$
\langle j_1 m_1\, j_2 m_2 | J M\rangle
= (-1)^{j_1+j_2-J}
\langle j_2 m_2\, j_1 m_1 | J M\rangle,
$$
$$
\langle j_1\,{-m_1}\; j_2\,{-m_2} | J\,{-M}\rangle
= (-1)^{j_1+j_2-J}
\langle j_1 m_1\, j_2 m_2 | J M\rangle,
$$
and for Wigner-$d$ at $\theta=\pi$ (needed for permutation rotations):
$$
d^{j}_{m'm}(\pi) = (-1)^{j-m}\,\delta_{m',-m}.
$$

## Convention registry

Every sign ambiguity encountered in this project should map to a row of this
table. When comparing two codes or two papers, walk the table top to bottom.

| Item | Choice used here | Where it enters |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| Wigner $D$ | $D^j_{m'm}(\alpha,\beta,\gamma)=e^{-im'\alpha}d^j_{m'm}(\beta)e^{-im\gamma}$ | all rotations |
| Clebsch–Gordan | Condon–Shortley | LS couplings, isospin |
| One-particle helicity state | $R(\phi,\theta,-\phi)$ vs $R(\phi,\theta,0)$ — **to fix** | fixed-frame amplitude phases |
| Jacob–Wick particle-2 | second particle via $(-1)^{s_2-\lambda_2}$-type phase (implicit in the recoupling formulas above) | $H_{\lambda_1\lambda_2}$, parity relation |
| DPD standard orientation | chain-1 alignment as in [@JPAC:2019ufm]; per-analysis choice noted in each case study | permutation phases |
| Parity intrinsic phases | PDG $\eta_P$ per species | parity relation |
| C phases of mesons | from interpolating currents, see `docs/isospin-kaon-conventions.qmd` | C constraints |
| Antiparticle spinors | $v(p,\lambda) = C\bar u^T(p,\lambda)$-type relation — **to fix** | tensor cross-checks with fermions |
| $\epsilon^{0123}$ sign | **to fix** | tensor amplitudes with $\epsilon_{\mu\nu\rho\sigma}$ |
| $\gamma_5$ sign | $\gamma_5 = i\gamma^0\gamma^1\gamma^2\gamma^3$ | tensor amplitudes |

::: {.callout-warning}
## To fix

The rows marked **to fix** must be pinned down (once) before quantitative
cross-checks between the helicity and tensor implementations are meaningful.
They are recorded in the [ledger](99-ledger.qmd).
:::
